codeReview.summary: Solid implementation of persistent 2048x2048 texture rendering with CPU-based pixel manipulation, Gaussian splat heatmap coloring, WiFiman color scheme, split-screen 40/60 layout, floating controls, and comprehensive test coverage of all pure logic. The worker chose CPU rendering over Metal compute shaders (documented deviation) which is reasonable for 2D incremental updates at 10Hz. All 12 expected behaviors are implemented. Six non-blocking issues found: unused Metal GPU objects wasting ~32MB VRAM, straight-vs-premultiplied alpha inconsistency, gesture handling that doesn't accumulate across sessions, heavy main-thread compositing loop, a potential crash in rasterizeFloorArea with reversed coordinates, and a minor test count discrepancy in the handoff.

issues.description: Metal objects allocated but unused. MTLDevice, MTLTexture (×2), and MTLCommandQueue are created in setupMetal() (lines 323-354) and cleaned up in cleanup() (lines 700-704), but never used for actual rendering. All pixel work uses CPU arrays (mapPixels, heatmapPixels, compositePixels). This wastes ~32MB of GPU memory (2 × 2048² × 4 bytes). Since the worker intentionally chose CPU rendering (documented in skillFeedback.deviations), these Metal objects should have been removed entirely.

Alpha blending mismatch. alphaBlend() performs straight (non-premultiplied) alpha compositing — it divides by outA on line 657-659, which is the straight alpha formula. However, createUIImage() creates a CGContext with CGImageAlphaInfo.premultipliedLast (line 687), which expects premultiplied pixel data. The pixel buffers store straight alpha but CGContext interprets them as premultiplied. For fully opaque regions this is invisible, but semi-transparent heatmap splat edges may render with slightly washed-out colors.

Pinch-to-zoom and pan gestures don't accumulate across gesture sessions. MagnificationGesture.onChanged directly assigns the raw gesture magnification to mapScale (line 169), and DragGesture.onChanged directly assigns value.translation to mapOffset (line 176). Each new gesture starts from scratch — the previous scale/offset baseline is lost. Standard practice is to track a base scale/offset and apply the delta on top. This will cause jumpy zoom/pan behavior: lifting a finger and starting a new pinch resets the zoom level.

renderFrame() iterates over all 4,194,304 pixels (2048² compositing loop at line 499, plus walking path drawing, position dot) on the main thread at 10Hz. While modern iOS devices can handle this, it's substantial main-thread work (~40M pixel operations/second). Combined with AR session updates, mesh processing, and UI rendering, this could cause occasional frame drops. Consider moving compositing to a background queue and dispatching the final UIImage to main.

rasterizeFloorArea uses ranges topLeft.texY...bottomRight.texY and topLeft.texX...bottomRight.texX (lines 409-410). If TextureCoordinateMapper returns topLeft > bottomRight (which could happen with certain map bounds configurations or inverted coordinate spaces), the closed range would be empty or crash. Should guard with min/max: for y in min(topLeft.texY, bottomRight.texY)...max(topLeft.texY, bottomRight.texY).
Handoff claims 22 HeatmapMetalRendererTests but actual count is 18 test functions. Minor documentation discrepancy — no missing test coverage, just an inaccurate count in the handoff summary.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker deviated from Metal compute shader approach to CPU-based rendering, which is a significant architectural decision. The rationale (CPU sufficient for 2D incremental updates at 10Hz, avoids .metal shader complexity) is documented in the handoff's skillFeedback but not captured in AGENTS.md as a project convention. Future workers building on this renderer may assume Metal GPU rendering is in use based on the file name and class structure.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: HeatmapMetalRenderer.swift creates Metal objects (lines 290-299, 323-354) but does all work on CPU arrays. Handoff skillFeedback.deviations[1]: 'Used CPU rendering into Metal persistent textures via MTLTexture.replace' — but even MTLTexture.replace is not actually used for rendering, only for initialization.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The ios-ui-worker skill file could not be found at .factory/skills/ios-ui-worker/SKILL.md. The worker referenced it in the feature config (skillName: 'ios-ui-worker') and the handoff mentions skill feedback, but the skill file doesn't exist in the expected location. Cannot verify if the worker followed the skill procedure.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Glob search for '*ios-ui-worker*' under .factory/ returned no results. Feature JSON has skillName: 'ios-ui-worker'.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker used rsync as a workaround for broken mac-mini git HTTPS authentication to sync files for remote test execution. This workaround is mentioned in discoveredIssues and skillFeedback.suggestedChanges but not documented in AGENTS.md's Known Pre-Existing Issues section as a workaround. The mac-mini git issue is documented there, but the rsync fallback approach is not, so future workers will rediscover it.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff discoveredIssues[1]: 'Pre-existing: mac-mini git HTTPS auth broken. Used rsync to sync files for remote test execution.' skillFeedback.suggestedChanges: 'Add rsync fallback note to skill when mac-mini git pull is broken'.

sharedStateObservations.observation: SwiftUI gesture handling pattern for pinch-to-zoom uses direct assignment rather than gesture-state accumulation. This creates a non-standard UX where zoom/pan resets between gesture sessions. No convention in AGENTS.md about preferred gesture implementation patterns for map interactions.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: ContinuousScanView.swift lines 167-178: MagnificationGesture.onChanged directly assigns to mapScale, DragGesture.onChanged directly assigns to mapOffset without preserving baseline values.

summary: PASS. The Metal rendering pipeline implementation is thorough and covers all 12 expected behaviors from the feature spec: persistent 2048x2048 textures, incremental updates, 10Hz render loop, real-time map growth, Gaussian splat heatmap, WiFiman colors, 40/60 split-screen, walking path, position dots on both views, pinch-to-zoom, auto-center viewport, and all floating controls. Test coverage is strong with 18 pure-logic tests and 40+ ViewModel state tests. Six non-blocking issues found: (1) unused Metal GPU objects wasting VRAM, (2) straight-vs-premultiplied alpha mismatch, (3) gesture handling that doesn't accumulate across sessions causing jumpy zoom/pan, (4) heavy main-thread compositing at 10Hz, (5) potential range crash in rasterizeFloorArea, (6) minor test count discrepancy in handoff. None are blocking — the feature is functional and well-structured.
